patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking office administrator. Start with: “I just can’t sleep lately.” Volunteer: trouble falling asleep most nights, waking multiple times and unable to return to sleep, tired/unrested mornings, for ~1 month. tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates evidence-based treatment for early-onset schizophrenia while explicitly meeting critical prescribing safety checks and monitoring requirements.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the word "schizophrenia" and frames it as a treatable condition with symptoms (e.g., hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thinking/behavior, negative symptoms) and functional impact.
Explains treatment using the word "antipsychotic" and describes goals (symptom reduction, relapse prevention, functional recovery) and realistic timeline for improvement.
Before prescribing, explicitly checks for medication allergies (including prior antipsychotic reactions) and documents/acknowledges the result.

Before prescribing, explicitly checks key contraindications/precautions relevant to antipsychotics (e.g., history of neuroleptic malignant syndrome, prolonged QT/arrhythmia risk, severe hepatic impairment considerations, pregnancy/lactation status when applicable, seizure risk, diabetes/metabolic risk, orthostatic hypotension risk).

Before prescribing, explicitly checks for drug-drug interactions (including QT-prolonging agents, CNS depressants, strong CYP inhibitors/inducers depending on chosen agent, and serotonergic/anticholinergic burden where relevant).
Uses a youth-appropriate approach: starts with a low dose, titrates cautiously, and avoids polypharmacy at initiation unless clearly justified.
Discusses and plans metabolic monitoring (weight/BMI, BP, glucose/HbA1c, lipids) and movement-disorder monitoring (EPS/akathisia) as part of antipsychotic initiation.
Assesses current safety risks (suicidality, self-harm, harm to others, inability to care for self) and provides clear escalation instructions if risk increases.
Provides shared decision-making: offers at least one reasonable antipsychotic option and incorporates patient/family preferences and prior response/tolerability when available.
Avoids unsafe claims (e.g., guarantees cure, advises stopping medication abruptly) and avoids prescribing without completing the required safety checks.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose early-onset schizophrenia and initiate a first-line oral second-generation antipsychotic with required safety checks and baseline monitoring plan.

medical/neurologic history (seizures, head injury, endocrine, cardiac history)

family history of psychotic or mood disorders

baseline metabolic status (weight/BMI; history of diabetes/dyslipidemia)

Explain treatment (include the term antipsychotic), expected benefits, and side effects; use shared decision-making to choose an agent appropriate for youth.
Complete allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check explicitly before prescribing.
Prescribe a single oral antipsychotic at a low starting dose with titration plan; provide counseling on adherence and adverse effects.
Outline monitoring plan: metabolic baseline and follow-up, EPS/akathisia checks, and safety plan for worsening symptoms or crisis.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of cluster headache (a trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia), enabling appropriate acute and preventive management planning.

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies the syndrome as cluster headache and explicitly frames it as a trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia (TAC).

Elicits core diagnostic features: strictly unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration attacks (typically 15–180 minutes), attack frequency (up to multiple per day), circadian/cluster periodicity, and ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or restlessness/agitation.
Assesses family history of similar attacks and clarifies that familial clustering can occur while still meeting TAC criteria.

Screens for red flags and secondary headache features (e.g., first/worst headache, thunderclap onset, neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, altered mental status, new onset after age 50, cancer/immunosuppression, pregnancy/postpartum, head trauma, jaw claudication/visual symptoms) and states when urgent evaluation is needed.
Provides patient-facing diagnosis explanation including the terms "cluster headache" and "trigeminal autonomic" in clear language.

Explains evidence-based acute treatment options including high-flow oxygen and a triptan (sumatriptan) as standard abortive therapies, while noting that treatment prescribing is not required for this L1 task.

If discussing or planning any medication, performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, contraindication check (e.g., cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension for triptans; smoking/fire risk for oxygen), and drug interaction check (e.g., triptans with ergotamines/MAO inhibitors, serotonergic agents).

Includes a brief differential (e.g., paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA, migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, sinusitis, secondary TAC from structural lesions) and justifies why cluster headache is most likely.

Advises follow-up and/or need for specialist evaluation if atypical features, abnormal neurologic exam, or poor response to standard therapy, and mentions consideration of neuroimaging when appropriate (new onset, atypical presentation, abnormal exam).

minimal_paths.description: Meets L1 requirements by collecting key TAC/cluster features, excluding major red flags, and recording the diagnosis as cluster headache (familial).

red flag screen (thunderclap, neuro deficits, fever/meningismus, new age >50, cancer/immunosuppression)
steps: Characterize headache phenotype (location, severity, duration, frequency, periodicity).
Ask about ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and restlessness/agitation during attacks.
Assess triggers (e.g., alcohol during bouts), prior similar bouts, and family history.
Perform targeted red-flag screen and brief neurologic review; determine if urgent evaluation is needed.
Explain diagnosis using the terms "cluster headache" and "trigeminal autonomic".
record_diagnosis with cluster headache (familial), trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical) and assesses contraindications to systemic steroids (critical), including active/untreated infection, uncontrolled diabetes, severe uncontrolled hypertension, active GI ulcer/bleeding risk, severe osteoporosis, psychiatric history (steroid-induced psychosis/mania), and pregnancy considerations.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical), specifically screening for NSAIDs/anticoagulants (GI bleed risk), CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors affecting steroid exposure, and live vaccines while immunosuppressed.

Addresses severity and red flags: evaluates for hemodynamic instability, chest pain/syncope, severe symptomatic anemia, or rapidly worsening hemolysis and advises urgent/emergency evaluation if present.

Counsels on steroid adverse effects and mitigation: hyperglycemia, mood/sleep changes, GI upset/ulcer risk, infection risk; advises taking with food and considering GI protection when risk factors exist.
Discusses monitoring plan in plain language: follow-up labs (CBC/reticulocyte count, bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin) and clinical symptom tracking to confirm reduced hemolysis.

Avoids unsafe recommendations: does not advise iron unless iron deficiency is demonstrated; does not recommend live vaccines during high-dose steroid therapy; does not delay treatment solely awaiting additional tests if presentation is consistent and patient is symptomatic.

Start with: “My vision gets messed up, then I blank out or jerk, and it’s happening more lately.” Volunteer only: shimmering/missing vision patches, confusion/word trouble for minutes, occasional right arm/leg twitching, severe headaches with nausea/light sensitivity, and that you were told you have polymicrogyria. Downplay frequency/impact because you fear losing agency shifts or being fired.

If asked directly, admit: odd bad smell or sudden fear before episodes, partner saw your eyes pull to one side, lingering reading/word-finding trouble, migraines since teens and family history with weakness, rare one-sided weakness after headache, learning/visual-spatial issues, sensitivity to flickering lights, sleep-onset jerks with tongue bite. Deny fever/head injury.

Do NOT mention unless specifically probed: left-side bumping/neglect, depth issues, brief staring spells, tingling, worse handwriting.

Resist/avoid: urine leakage, avoiding driving, “not real” feeling, alcohol/cannabis use.

Express misconceptions: worry about PANDAS/OCD after sore throats; others call it panic attacks; wonder about Rubinstein-Taybi. Argue that generic swaps are dangerous, vision monitoring for vigabatrin is optional/too expensive, and you may stretch meds to save money.

If the clinician is empathetic, soften slightly but stay guarded; you may reveal one resisted detail only after reassurance about confidentiality, safety, and costs.

MRI Brain (3T, epilepsy protocol).normal_range: Normal cortical gyration and gray-white differentiation

medications: Sumatriptan 50 mg orally as needed for migraine (max 200 mg/day)
comorbidities: Focal epilepsy secondary to cortical malformation

THC use potentially contributing to dizziness, anxiety, and altered perception of auras
Mild hypomagnesemia lowering seizure threshold and worsening migraine frequency
Vigabatrin-associated visual field constriction complicating assessment of occipital symptoms

Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to EP300 haploinsufficiency

Neurodevelopmental phenotype consistent with malformation of cortical development
Seizure history compatible with structural epilepsy

medications: Vigabatrin titrate up to 100-150 mg/kg/day PO divided BID as needed/tolerated
non_pharmacological: Baseline ophthalmology exam and visual field/retinal monitoring plan
Brain MRI review and epilepsy syndrome characterization
EEG to assess seizure burden and guide antiseizure strategy
Genetic testing: chromosomal microarray and targeted sequencing (CREBBP, EP300)
Early intervention therapies (PT/OT/speech) and neurodevelopmental support
Seizure action plan and caregiver education on seizure first aid

general_concerns: Thinks scleroderma is the same as a contagious skin infection because of the word "sclero" and worries about passing it to students.
Believes that if she avoids all cold exposure and stress, the disease will stop progressing entirely.
Assumes that tightening skin means the problem is only cosmetic and doesn’t realize it can involve organs even when skin changes seem mild.
Thinks acid-related products like benzoic acid in foods or skincare might be causing the condition or making it flare, and wants to eliminate anything "acidic."
Believes that once symptoms improve, she can stop follow-up visits and monitoring because the disease is "gone."
economic_concerns: Worries that frequent specialist visits and tests will be unaffordable and asks if she can do only "essential" monitoring.
Concerned about missing work for appointments and losing income from using unpaid leave days.
Thinks brand-name medications are always necessary and assumes she can’t afford treatment if generics exist but are "weaker."

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 44-year-old female elementary school teacher. Be cooperative and clear. Start with: “My fingers keep turning white and hurting in the cold, and my skin feels tight.” Early in the visit, volunteer: fingers turn white/blue with cold or stress then throb/tingle on warming; finger skin feels tight/shiny and harder to bend; morning hand puffiness; frequent heartburn, worse after meals or lying down. If asked, add: food sometimes feels stuck and needs water; slow-healing fingertip sores; hand joint aches/stiffness in mornings; new shortness of breath on stairs and intermittent dry cough; dry mouth needing water to talk; fingers don’t open as wide and you drop things. Do not mention unless specifically asked: tiny red dots on face/hands; smaller mouth opening; numb fingertips even when normal-looking; a few recent “high” BP readings; bloating/gas and occasional loose stools. Only reveal painful sex due to dryness if directly asked; be slightly hesitant. Share misconceptions: think it’s poor circulation/anemia, maybe carpal tunnel, and heartburn from spicy food/antacids. If clinician is empathetic, acknowledge and open up more. Emphasize cost/work concerns; ask for “essential” tests and generic options.

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Benzoic acid topical 6% apply to affected skin BID
confounders: Tobacco use (smokes ~0.5 pack/day) worsening Raynaud symptoms and digital ischemia risk
Occupational cold exposure (works in refrigerated food storage) triggering vasospasm
Chronic proton pump inhibitor use potentially contributing to low iron/micronutrient deficiency and fatigue
Intermittent NSAID use for arthralgias complicating renal function assessment in the setting of possible scleroderma renal involvement

required_evidence: positive ANA or Scl-70 or anticentromere

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: often normal; may be low if chronic disease or GI bleeding
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: usually normal; may be low with iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: normal unless renal involvement (elevated with renal crisis/AKI)

non_pharmacological: screen organ involvement (PFTs, echocardiogram, renal monitoring)
